wiki_catalogs.name: Project Overview
wiki_catalogs.prompt: Create comprehensive content for this section focused on the el_polo_loco game project. Explain its purpose as a 2D side-scrolling browser-based game built with vanilla JavaScript and HTML5 Canvas. Describe the core gameplay mechanics including character movement, jumping, throwing projectiles, enemy combat, and endboss progression. Document the technology stack, emphasizing the use of pure vanilla JS without frameworks or build tools. Illustrate the high-level architecture showing how the game initializes via index.html, loads dependencies in order, and starts the game loop through the World class. Include a brief explanation of the file structure (js/, levels/, models/) and their roles. Provide context on how the game integrates user input, rendering, physics, and UI elements into a cohesive experience. Use terminology consistent with the codebase such as 'World', 'Character', 'ThrowableObject', and 'StatusBar'. Include a simple diagram showing the main components and their relationships. Address common beginner questions about how to run the game and what prerequisites are needed.

wiki_catalogs.name: Movement Mechanics
wiki_catalogs.prompt: Develop detailed content for the character's movement mechanics including left/right translation and jump functionality. Explain how input from the Keyboard class is processed in the animation loop to trigger moveLeft() and moveRight() methods, and how the camera_x offset is adjusted to create the side-scrolling effect. Detail the jump() method's interaction with the physics system via speedY and acceleration in applyGravity(). Include code examples showing the 60fps interval-based movement updates and boundary checks against levelEndX and world limits. Describe how the otherDirection flag controls sprite flipping for visual orientation. Address common issues such as input lag, movement clipping, and camera desynchronization, and provide solutions like precise interval timing and coordinate clamping. Make the content accessible to beginners while including technical depth on coordinate transformation and input handling for advanced developers.

wiki_catalogs.name: Throwable Objects
wiki_catalogs.prompt: Develop detailed content for the ThrowableObject class in el_polo_loco. Explain its inheritance from MovableObjects and how it implements projectile mechanics using gravity, animation, and directional movement. Document the constructor's initialization logic, including positioning relative to the character's offset and direction. Describe the throw() method's implementation of horizontal velocity based on character orientation and the application of gravity. Detail the animate() method's role in switching between rotation and splash animations based on ground collision. Include code examples showing how bottle trajectory is calculated and how sprite scaling is dynamically adjusted. Address common issues such as incorrect positioning due to rectOffset values, animation flickering, and memory leaks from setInterval. Provide guidance on extending the system with new throwable types by modifying image sets or adding physics variations.

wiki_catalogs.name: Game Loop
wiki_catalogs.prompt: Create architectural documentation for the game loop system in el_polo_loco. Explain the dual-timing mechanism using requestAnimationFrame for rendering and setInterval for collision/throw detection. Detail how the draw() method recursively calls itself via requestAnimationFrame to maintain 60fps rendering, while separate intervals at 200ms handle game logic updates. Describe the coordination between visual updates and state changes, including canvas clearing, coordinate translation for camera movement, and object rendering order. Document performance implications of this approach, such as frame consistency, garbage collection of off-screen throwable objects, and potential jank from long-running operations. Include optimization strategies like minimizing layout thrashing and efficient redraw regions. Provide code examples showing the integration between World.draw() and requestAnimationFrame, and explain how the game loop ensures smooth animation and responsive gameplay.

wiki_catalogs.name: Status Bars
wiki_catalogs.prompt: Develop detailed content for the status bar implementation in the game, focusing on the StatusBar class that extends DrawableObject. Explain how the class manages visual representation of player health, bottle count, coin count, and endboss health using image-based percentage indicators. Document the constructor's role in initializing the correct image based on the statusbar type and position. Describe the setPosition() method that dynamically adjusts coordinates and dimensions for each status bar element (background, fill, icon) based on its type and category. Detail how setCharacter() establishes a reference to the player character and initializes the width multiplier for energy calculation. Explain the setInterval-driven setWidth() logic that continuously updates the health bar width based on current character energy. Include practical examples from the code showing how imagesHealthBar, imagesBottleBar, and imagesHealthBarEndboss arrays define the visual assets. Address common issues such as delayed character initialization due to setTimeout, misaligned UI elements, and incorrect scaling calculations. Provide guidance on modifying bar dimensions, changing asset paths, and adding new status bar types.

wiki_catalogs.name: Level Configuration
wiki_catalogs.prompt: Develop detailed content for the level configuration system. Explain how the Level class serves as a data container that organizes game elements into structured arrays: clouds, enemies, backgroundObjects, and statusBars. Document the constructor parameters and how they are assigned to instance properties. Describe how level1.js instantiates the Level class with specific arrays of game objects, defining the composition and layout of the game world. Provide examples from the code showing how enemies like Chicken and Endboss are instantiated and grouped, and how status bars are configured for health, bottles, coins, and endboss health. Explain the significance of the levelEndX property in determining level boundaries. Include guidance on creating new levels by modifying object arrays or extending with additional entities. Address common configuration issues such as missing object types, incorrect array ordering, and synchronization between UI elements and game logic.

wiki_catalogs.name: DrawableObject Base Class
wiki_catalogs.prompt: Create comprehensive documentation for the DrawableObject class, the foundational base class for all visual entities in the game. Explain its role in managing image loading (loadImage, loadImages), rendering (draw), and debugging visualization (drawFrame, drawCollisionFrame). Detail how it provides shared properties like x, y, width, height, and imageCache for sprite management. Include examples of how child classes inherit and utilize these methods. Document the purpose of the drawFrame and drawCollisionFrame methods in rendering debug overlays for hitboxes and collision frames, and how they conditionally render based on object type. Address performance considerations in image caching and canvas drawing operations. Provide code examples showing image preloading and frame rendering from actual game implementation.

wiki_catalogs.name: DrawableObject Class Reference
wiki_catalogs.prompt: Create API documentation for the DrawableObject class, which serves as the base class for all visual elements in the el_polo_loco game. Document the constructor, public properties (x, y, width, height, img, imageCache, currentImage), and methods including loadImage(path), loadImages(arr), draw(ctx), drawFrame(ctx), and drawCollisionFrame(ctx). Explain the purpose of each method and property, including how image loading and caching works. Detail the coordinate system and Canvas rendering process. Provide usage examples showing how to instantiate and render a DrawableObject. Explain the role of drawFrame and drawCollisionFrame in debugging collision zones, including conditional rendering logic based on instance type. Document inheritance expectations and how child classes should interact with the base rendering methods.

wiki_catalogs.name: Getting Started
wiki_catalogs.prompt: Develop detailed content for setting up and running the el_polo_loco game. Provide step-by-step instructions for launching the game by opening index.html in a modern browser, with no build process or server required. Explain the role of each script tag in index.html and why loading order matters due to class dependencies. Guide users through the initialization sequence starting from the body onload='init()' call, creation of the canvas element, instantiation of the World and Keyboard classes, and binding of keyboard event listeners. Include a working example of how to trigger character movement and throwing actions using arrow keys and spacebar. Document common issues such as script loading errors due to incorrect paths or missing files, and provide troubleshooting steps. Highlight the importance of strict mode in 1-game.js and how it affects code execution. Make this section beginner-friendly by avoiding deep technical jargon while still being precise about implementation details.

wiki_catalogs.name: Animation States
wiki_catalogs.prompt: Develop detailed content for the character's state-based animation system. Explain how the animate() method uses setInterval to check keyboard input and character state to determine the appropriate animation sequence (idle, walking, jumping, throwing, hurt, dead). Detail the image arrays (imagesIdle, imagesWalk, etc.) and how playAnimation() cycles through them using currentImage modulo arithmetic. Describe the transition logic between states, including the sleep timer that switches to long idle after 3 seconds of inactivity. Include practical examples from the code showing image loading via loadImage() and loadImages(), and how sprite sheets are selected based on game conditions. Address common issues like animation flickering, incorrect state transitions, and image caching problems, and provide debugging strategies such as state logging and frame rate monitoring. Cover optimization tips for smooth animation playback and memory-efficient image handling.

wiki_catalogs.name: Collision Detection
wiki_catalogs.prompt: Develop detailed content for the collision detection system in el_polo_loco. Explain how World.checkCollisions() iterates through enemies and uses MovableObjects.isColliding() to detect player-enemy contact. Document the logic that prevents damage during invincibility frames via character.isHurt(). Describe the hit() method's implementation of energy reduction and timestamp tracking for hurt state management. Include code examples showing the exact collision conditions and energy decrement behavior. Detail how collision boundaries are calculated using object dimensions and position offsets. Address common issues such as inaccurate hitboxes, false collision triggers, and timing issues with hurt states. Provide optimization suggestions for scaling collision detection to larger enemy counts and recommendations for implementing projectile-enemy collision in future extensions.

wiki_catalogs.name: Object Composition
wiki_catalogs.prompt: Develop detailed content for the object composition pattern used in the World class. Explain how the World acts as a central game coordinator that aggregates and manages all game objects including Character, enemies, status bars, clouds, background objects, and throwable objects. Describe the setWorld() method that injects world context into individual objects, enabling cross-object communication. Detail how the draw() method orchestrates rendering by iterating through object collections via addObjectsToMap() and addToMap(). Discuss the benefits of composition over inheritance in this context, such as flexibility in object configuration and dynamic level construction. Illustrate with code examples how the level configuration from level1.js is integrated into the World instance. Address common issues like object lifecycle management and memory leaks, particularly with dynamically created throwable objects, and provide best practices for object disposal and reference cleanup.

wiki_catalogs.name: Health Management
wiki_catalogs.prompt: Develop detailed content for the health management system that integrates Character and StatusBar classes. Explain how the character's energy property is decremented on collision with enemies via the hit() method and how this value drives the visual update of the health bar. Document the data flow from character.hit() → StatusBar.setWidth() → visual rendering, emphasizing the 100ms interval update mechanism. Describe how the multiplier property in StatusBar calculates the pixel-to-energy ratio for accurate bar scaling. Illustrate with code examples how the health bar's fill portion (type 1) is updated while the background (type 0) and icon (type 2) remain static. Explain the delayed initialization pattern using setTimeout to ensure world object availability before accessing world.character. Address common issues such as health bar not updating, incorrect energy display after damage, and synchronization delays between game state and UI. Provide troubleshooting steps for debugging health-related UI issues and optimization tips for reducing update frequency or implementing event-driven updates instead of polling.

wiki_catalogs.name: Background Rendering
wiki_catalogs.prompt: Develop detailed content for the background rendering system. Explain how BackgroundObject instances in level1.js are positioned at specific x-coordinates (-1440, -720, 0, 720, etc.) to create a seamless, repeating parallax effect across the scrolling game world. Document how the World class uses canvas translation in the draw() method to shift the coordinate system based on camera_x, enabling the illusion of movement. Describe the layered structure of the background: air, third_layer, second_layer, and first_layer, and how their rendering order creates depth. Include examples from the code showing how multiple background segments are instantiated to cover the entire level width. Explain how the addObjectsToMap() method renders all background objects in sequence. Address performance considerations when adding more background layers or increasing level length, and provide best practices for asset sizing and memory usage. Illustrate how to modify or extend the background with new images or layer configurations.

wiki_catalogs.name: MovableObjects Intermediate Class
wiki_catalogs.prompt: Develop detailed documentation for the MovableObjects class that extends DrawableObject and introduces physics, movement, and collision capabilities. Explain its implementation of gravity (applyGravity, isAboveGround), directional movement (moveLeft, moveRight), jumping mechanics, and collision detection (isColliding). Document the energy-based health system (hit, isHurt, isDead) and animation handling (playAnimation). Describe the use of rectOffset properties for precise collision boundaries. Include practical examples from the codebase showing how setInterval drives continuous movement and animation. Address common issues like floating-point precision in position updates, timing conflicts between game loops, and collision detection accuracy. Provide performance optimization tips for reducing interval overhead and improving collision checks.

wiki_catalogs.name: MovableObjects Class Reference
wiki_catalogs.prompt: Create comprehensive API documentation for the MovableObjects class, which extends DrawableObject and provides physics, animation, and collision capabilities for all moving entities. Document all properties including speedY, acceleration, energy, lastHit, and rectOffset values. Detail methods such as applyGravity(groundLevel), isAboveGround(groundLevel), isColliding(mo), hit(), isHurt(), isDead(), playAnimation(arr), moveRight(), moveLeft(directionState), and jump(). Explain the gravity implementation using setInterval and velocity calculations. Describe the collision detection algorithm with offset-aware bounding boxes. Document the animation system using image arrays and modulo indexing. Provide examples of how to use playAnimation with sprite sequences. Explain the purpose of rectOffset properties in fine-tuning collision detection. Include information on how this class serves as the foundation for Character, Chicken, Endboss, and ThrowableObject.

wiki_catalogs.name: Core Architecture
wiki_catalogs.prompt: Create architectural documentation for the el_polo_loco game's core structure. Describe the object-oriented design pattern using class inheritance with DrawableObject as the base class, extended by MovableObjects, and further specialized into Character, Chicken, Endboss, and ThrowableObject. Explain the composition pattern in the World class that aggregates all game objects (character, enemies, status bars, throwable objects) and manages their coordination. Detail the game loop implementation using requestAnimationFrame for rendering and setInterval for collision/throw checks. Document the data flow from user input (Keyboard class) to game state changes in World and visual updates on canvas. Illustrate the coordinate system transformation used for camera following via ctx.translate(). Address the technical decisions behind using vanilla JS without frameworks, the trade-offs involved, and scalability limitations. Include a component diagram showing class relationships and method call sequences. Discuss performance considerations such as animation frame rate, garbage collection of thrown bottles, and efficient canvas clearing.

wiki_catalogs.name: Physics Implementation
wiki_catalogs.prompt: Develop detailed content for the character's physics system, focusing on gravity, collision, and energy management. Explain how applyGravity() in MovableObjects creates realistic falling motion using speedY and acceleration updated at 60fps, and how isAboveGround() determines contact with the ground level. Detail the energy system including the hit() method that reduces energy by 10 points per hit and sets lastHit timestamp, and how isHurt() and isDead() methods use this data for state checks. Describe the rectOffset values that define precise collision boxes and prevent sprite-edge false collisions. Include code examples showing the integration between Character and MovableObjects physics methods. Address common issues such as floating characters, inaccurate collision detection, and energy state desync, and provide solutions like boundary debugging and timestamp validation. Include performance considerations for efficient physics calculations and collision detection optimization.

wiki_catalogs.name: Enemy Interactions
wiki_catalogs.prompt: Develop detailed content for enemy interaction mechanics in el_polo_loco. Explain how throwable objects interact with enemies through collision detection, even though direct enemy damage logic isn't implemented in the current codebase. Document the existing framework that could support enemy damage via throwable collisions and suggest implementation patterns based on the character.hit() method. Describe how the World class coordinates interactions between throwableObjects and enemies arrays. Include code examples showing potential extensions for detecting bottle-enemy collisions and applying damage. Address design considerations for balancing enemy health, projectile effectiveness, and spawn mechanics. Provide troubleshooting guidance for common interaction bugs such as missed collisions or improper damage application, and recommend testing strategies for validating combat mechanics.

wiki_catalogs.name: Inheritance Hierarchy
wiki_catalogs.prompt: Create comprehensive documentation for the class inheritance hierarchy in el_polo_loco. Start with DrawableObject as the base class providing core rendering capabilities (loadImage, draw, drawFrame), then explain how MovableObjects extends it with physics, animation, and collision detection. Detail how Character, Chicken, Endboss, and ThrowableObject further specialize MovableObjects with unique behaviors. Use code examples to show method inheritance and overriding, such as playAnimation() and isColliding(). Document the initialization flow from constructor chaining to asset loading. Explain the design rationale behind this inheritance model, including code reuse, polymorphism in draw() calls, and shared collision logic. Address potential pitfalls like tight coupling and deep inheritance trees, and suggest extension patterns for new game entities. Include a UML-style diagram in text form showing the class relationships and method/property inheritance.

wiki_catalogs.name: Score Tracking
wiki_catalogs.prompt: Develop detailed content for the score tracking mechanism using bottle and coin collection systems. Explain how collected bottles and coins are tracked within the Character class and reflected in their respective status bars (imagesBottleBar and imagesCoinBar). Document how the status bar system currently lacks dynamic updating for bottle and coin counts, relying only on static image loading based on type, and analyze the implications for gameplay feedback. Describe the current implementation gap where only health bars have dynamic width updates while bottle and coin bars are not linked to actual inventory values. Provide guidance on extending the StatusBar class to support dynamic updating for non-health metrics by implementing setInterval or event-driven updates similar to setWidth(). Include code examples showing how to modify the constructor and add update logic for bottle and coin counts. Address common implementation challenges such as managing multiple interval timers, preventing memory leaks, and synchronizing UI updates with game events. Recommend best practices for refactoring the system to use a unified update mechanism across all status metrics.

wiki_catalogs.name: Object Placement
wiki_catalogs.prompt: Develop detailed content for the object placement system. Explain how game objects such as enemies, clouds, and status bars are positioned in world coordinates within the level configuration. Document how the Level class aggregates these objects into arrays that are accessed by the World during rendering and collision detection. Describe how Chicken and Endboss instances are placed along the x-axis to create combat challenges at specific locations. Explain how clouds are distributed to enhance visual atmosphere without gameplay impact. Detail how status bars are instantiated with type identifiers ('imagesHealthBar', 'imagesBottleBar', etc.) and position indices to control their screen placement. Illustrate how the World class iterates through these arrays during the draw() cycle and integrates them into the game loop. Provide practical examples showing how to adjust enemy density, reposition UI elements, or balance encounter spacing. Address common issues such as overlapping objects, incorrect rendering order, and performance degradation from excessive entity counts.

wiki_catalogs.name: Character Class (Player Entity)
wiki_catalogs.prompt: Create detailed documentation for the Character class that represents the player-controlled entity. Explain its inheritance from MovableObjects and extension with player-specific behaviors including movement controls (keyboard input handling), animation states (idle, walking, jumping, throwing, hurt, dead), and sleep/idle detection logic. Document the constructor's initialization of image assets and animation intervals. Detail how the animate() method coordinates multiple setInterval loops for movement, camera tracking, and state-based animation sequencing. Explain the relationship between keyboard input (world.keyboard) and character actions. Include examples of animation state transitions based on input and physics state. Address implementation challenges like input debouncing, animation synchronization, and camera offset calculation. Provide guidance on extending the class with new abilities or animations.

wiki_catalogs.name: Character Class Reference
wiki_catalogs.prompt: Create detailed API documentation for the Character class, which represents the player-controlled entity. Document all properties including dimensions, groundLevel, speed, animation state flags, and sprite image arrays (imagesIdle, imagesWalk, imagesJump, etc.). Explain the constructor's responsibilities: loading all animation sprites, applying gravity, and starting the animation loop. Detail the animate() method's implementation with multiple setInterval calls for movement input processing, position updates, camera tracking, and animation state selection. Document how keyboard input from the World object controls movement and actions. Explain the sleep/idle animation transition logic with timer resets. Provide examples of how animation states are selected based on input and game conditions. Include guidance on extending the class with new animation states or modifying movement behavior while maintaining compatibility with the World and collision systems.

wiki_catalogs.name: Game Initialization
wiki_catalogs.prompt: Develop detailed content for the game initialization process. Thoroughly explain how the HTML page loads all required JavaScript files in dependency order, ensuring parent classes are defined before child classes. Walk through the init() function in 1-game.js that retrieves the canvas element, creates a new World instance with canvas and keyboard references, and sets up global access via window.world. Document the World constructor's responsibilities: setting up the 2D rendering context, initializing game state variables (camera_x, throwableObjects, lastThrow), and starting the game loop via draw() and run(). Explain how the keyboard input system is instantiated and bound to window events for keydown and keyup. Include code examples showing the exact sequence of operations and object creation. Address common initialization issues such as null canvas references, script loading order errors, and timing problems with object initialization. Provide best practices for extending this initialization pattern with additional game elements.

wiki_catalogs.name: Camera System
wiki_catalogs.prompt: Develop detailed content for the camera following system in el_polo_loco. Explain how the camera_x property in the World class enables side-scrolling by translating the canvas context relative to the player's x-position. Detail the implementation in World.draw() using ctx.translate(this.camera_x, 0) and its inverse operation. Show how the character's position is used to calculate camera_x in the animation loop via world.camera_x = -this.x + 100. Discuss coordinate system transformations and their impact on object rendering order and layering. Address edge cases like boundary limits (level start/end) and performance considerations of frequent canvas state saves/restores. Provide examples of how background objects remain static while foreground elements scroll, creating a parallax effect. Include troubleshooting guidance for common camera issues such as jitter, incorrect offsets, and rendering artifacts.

wiki_catalogs.name: Enemy Classes (Chicken and Endboss)
wiki_catalogs.prompt: Develop comprehensive documentation for the enemy entity classes: Chicken and Endboss. Explain how both inherit from MovableObjects and implement autonomous behavior through animation loops and movement patterns. For Chicken, document its randomized spawning (x position, speed), leftward movement, and walk animation cycle. For Endboss, detail its fixed position, alert and walk animations, and future extension points for boss behavior. Compare their implementation approaches and highlight shared patterns in enemy AI. Include code examples showing constructor initialization, image loading, and animation setup. Address common development issues like enemy pathing limitations, animation timing, and scalability for additional enemy types. Provide guidance on creating new enemy variants by extending MovableObjects with custom behavior.

wiki_catalogs.name: Chicken Class Reference
wiki_catalogs.prompt: Create API documentation for the Chicken class, representing basic enemy units. Document properties including dimensions, groundLevel, random spawn positioning, and variable speed. Explain the constructor's implementation: randomizing starting position and movement speed, loading walk animation sprites, and initializing animation. Detail the animate() method that handles continuous leftward movement using moveLeft(false) and animation cycling via playAnimation(imagesWalk). Describe the enemy's behavior pattern and movement constraints. Provide examples of how the Chicken class inherits physics and collision capabilities from MovableObjects. Include information on potential extensions such as adding attack behaviors, different movement patterns, or drop mechanics upon defeat.

wiki_catalogs.name: Character System
wiki_catalogs.prompt: Create comprehensive content for the Character class which represents the player-controlled entity. Explain its implementation as a subclass of MovableObjects with specific animation states (idle, walking, jumping, hurt, dead) and movement mechanics (left, right, jump, throw). Detail the physics implementation including gravity application, ground collision detection, and energy management for health. Document the state-based animation system that selects sprite sheets based on current actions. Describe how the character interacts with other game systems: receiving input from Keyboard, triggering throwable object creation, detecting collisions with enemies, and updating status bars. Include practical examples from the code showing how animation images are loaded and cycled. Address common issues like animation glitches, collision detection inaccuracies, and state transition bugs. Provide optimization tips for smooth character movement and responsive controls.

wiki_catalogs.name: Environment Classes (BackgroundObject and Clouds)
wiki_catalogs.prompt: Create detailed documentation for the environment and decorative classes: BackgroundObject and Clouds. Explain their role in creating the game's visual backdrop and parallax effects. Document how BackgroundObject extends MovableObjects to position static background elements using imagePath and x coordinate parameters in its constructor. Describe how these objects leverage inherited rendering capabilities without needing physics or collision. Include implementation details about canvas layering and coordinate management for background consistency. Address how these classes contribute to the overall visual composition while maintaining performance through simple rendering. Provide examples of level integration from level1.js and explain extension possibilities for animated backgrounds or dynamic weather effects.

wiki_catalogs.name: Endboss Class Reference
wiki_catalogs.prompt: Create API documentation for the Endboss class, representing the final enemy in the game. Document properties including dimensions, groundLevel, fixed starting position, and animation sequences (imagesAlert, imagesWalk). Explain the constructor's responsibilities: initializing with walk animation and starting the animation loop. Detail the animate() method that cycles through walk animations using setInterval. Describe the current implementation limitations and potential extension points for adding alert states, attack patterns, health tracking, and defeat animations. Explain how the Endboss inherits core functionality from MovableObjects while serving as a specialized enemy type. Provide guidance on implementing advanced behaviors such as player tracking, phase transitions, and special attacks while maintaining performance and collision integrity.

wiki_catalogs.name: Combat System
wiki_catalogs.prompt: Develop detailed content for the combat mechanics in el_polo_loco. Explain how players throw bottles using the spacebar to create new ThrowableObject instances that move across the screen. Document the throw interval logic in World.checkThrowableObject() that prevents spamming by enforcing a 1-second cooldown. Describe the collision detection system in World.checkCollisions() that checks if the character intersects with enemies and applies damage when appropriate. Detail the ThrowableObject class implementation including its trajectory, animation, and interaction with the game world. Explain how enemy health is managed through the hit() method and energy depletion. Include code examples showing the exact conditions for successful hits and damage application. Address common combat issues such as inaccurate collision boxes, projectile lifetime management, and enemy respawning behavior. Provide suggestions for balancing combat difficulty and extending the system with new weapon types.

wiki_catalogs.name: BackgroundObject Class Reference
wiki_catalogs.prompt: Create API documentation for the BackgroundObject class, which extends MovableObjects to represent static background elements. Document the fixed dimensions (720x480) and constructor parameters (imagePath, x) that allow instantiation with specific images and horizontal positioning. Explain how it inherits rendering capabilities from DrawableObject while maintaining its position. Describe its role in creating parallax scrolling effects when combined with camera movement. Provide usage examples showing how multiple BackgroundObject instances are arranged to create seamless backgrounds. Include information on potential extensions such as adding parallax layers with different scroll speeds or animated background elements.

wiki_catalogs.name: UI System
wiki_catalogs.prompt: Create comprehensive content for the UI components in the game, focusing on the StatusBar class and its integration with the World. Explain how multiple status bars display player health, collected bottles, coins, and endboss health using percentage-based visual indicators. Document the rendering process in World.draw() that translates coordinates to position status bars correctly on screen. Describe how the StatusBar class updates its visual representation based on changing game state values from Character and other entities. Include examples showing how the percentage value maps to filled portion of the bar. Explain the drawFrame() and drawCollisionFrame() methods used for debugging visual boundaries. Address common UI issues such as misaligned bars, incorrect percentage calculations, and visibility problems during gameplay. Provide guidance on customizing the appearance of status bars and adding new metrics like score or timer.

wiki_catalogs.name: StatusBar Class Reference
wiki_catalogs.prompt: Create comprehensive API documentation for the StatusBar class, which displays player and boss health, bottle count, and coin collection. Document all image arrays (imagesHealthBar, imagesBottleBar, etc.), positioning properties (x, y, width, height, gap), and constructor parameters (statusbar, type). Explain the setCharacter() method that asynchronously links to the World's character instance and initializes the energy multiplier. Detail the setWidth() method that dynamically updates the bar width based on character energy. Describe the setPosition() method's complex logic for placing different bar components (empty bar, filled bar, icon) in correct screen locations. Document the three status bar types and their visual representations. Provide examples of how to create health, bottle, and coin bars, including the special endboss health bar positioning. Include troubleshooting guidance for common issues like undefined character references or misaligned components.

wiki_catalogs.name: Level System
wiki_catalogs.prompt: Develop detailed content for the level configuration and rendering system. Explain how level1.js defines the game world layout by instantiating arrays of background objects, clouds, enemies, and status bars. Document how the Level class (if present) or direct object arrays are used by the World to render parallax backgrounds and place interactive elements. Describe the coordinate-based placement system that positions objects at specific x,y coordinates to create the scrolling environment. Explain how different object types contribute to gameplay: background for visuals, clouds for atmosphere, enemies for combat challenges. Include examples showing how to modify existing levels or create new ones by adjusting object positions and counts. Address common level design issues such as object density, performance impact of too many entities, and proper spacing between enemies. Provide best practices for designing balanced levels with progressive difficulty.

wiki_catalogs.name: ThrowableObject Class Reference
wiki_catalogs.prompt: Create detailed API documentation for the ThrowableObject class, representing projectiles (salsa bottles) thrown by the player. Document properties including dimensions, groundLevel, and sprite arrays (imagesRotate, imagesSplash). Explain the constructor's implementation: receiving a character reference, positioning based on character state, loading animation sprites, and immediately starting animation and throwing mechanics. Detail the throw(x, y) method that calculates trajectory based on character direction, applies initial velocity and gravity, and sets horizontal movement. Describe the animate() method that switches between rotation and splash animations based on ground collision. Explain the physics implementation using applyGravity() and frame-based position updates. Document the image scaling during animation (0.2x natural size). Provide examples of how throwable objects are instantiated and managed in the World's throwableObjects array. Include guidance on extending the class with different projectile types or explosion effects.

wiki_catalogs.name: Camera System
wiki_catalogs.prompt: Create comprehensive content for the camera following mechanism in the game. Explain how the World class uses the camera_x property to create a side-scrolling effect by translating the canvas context relative to the character's position. Detail the implementation in World.draw() that applies ctx.translate(this.camera_x, 0) before drawing world objects and restores it afterward. Describe how camera_x is updated based on character movement to keep the player centered (except at level boundaries). Explain the coordinate transformation logic that ensures objects move relative to the camera while the character stays near the center of the screen. Include code examples showing how background objects maintain parallax effects through different translation speeds. Address common camera issues such as jittery movement, incorrect translation boundaries, and objects disappearing at screen edges. Provide optimization techniques for smooth camera panning and edge-case handling.

wiki_catalogs.name: Keyboard Class Reference
wiki_catalogs.prompt: Create API documentation for the Keyboard class, which manages user input state. Document all boolean properties (LEFT, RIGHT, UP, DOWN, SPACE, ANY) that represent key press states. Explain its role as an observable state container rather than an event handler - actual event binding occurs in 1-game.js. Describe how the World and Character classes poll these boolean flags to determine player actions. Explain the ANY property's purpose in detecting general input activity (used for interrupting idle animations). Provide examples of how keyboard state is checked in game loops. Include implementation details on how event listeners in 1-game.js update these properties on keydown and keyup events. Document best practices for extending the class with additional controls or gamepad support while maintaining compatibility with existing input logic.

wiki_catalogs.name: Class Hierarchy
wiki_catalogs.prompt: Create comprehensive documentation for the entire class inheritance structure in the game. Start with DrawableObject as the base class providing fundamental rendering methods (draw, drawFrame, drawCollisionFrame) and image management. Then document MovableObjects as the intermediate class adding physics properties (speed, acceleration, gravity), movement methods, and collision detection. Detail how all game entities inherit from these: Character (player), Chicken and Endboss (enemies), ThrowableObject (projectiles), Clouds and BackgroundObject (decorative elements). Explain the benefits of this inheritance hierarchy in reducing code duplication and maintaining consistent behavior across objects. Include UML-style diagrams showing the full inheritance tree with methods and properties at each level. Provide code examples demonstrating polymorphic behavior in the addObjectsToMap() method. Address common inheritance issues such as method overriding conflicts, property shadowing, and tight coupling between classes.

wiki_catalogs.name: Level Class Reference
wiki_catalogs.prompt: Create API documentation for the Level class, which encapsulates all game world elements for a specific stage. Document all properties: clouds, enemies, backgroundObjects, statusBars, and levelEndX (fixed at 2160). Explain the constructor parameters that allow composition of a level from arrays of game objects. Describe how the class serves as a data container rather than having active methods. Detail its role in the World's rendering and collision systems. Provide examples of level composition using the level1.js configuration. Explain the significance of levelEndX in constraining player movement. Include guidance on creating new levels by instantiating the Level class with custom object arrays. Document the expected structure of each object array and how they integrate with the game's rendering pipeline and camera system.

wiki_catalogs.name: Game Loop
wiki_catalogs.prompt: Develop detailed content for the game loop implementation that drives all real-time interactions. Explain the dual-loop system: requestAnimationFrame for continuous rendering (60fps) in World.draw(), and setInterval for periodic game logic updates (5fps) in World.run(). Document how the draw() method clears the canvas, applies camera translation, renders all objects in correct z-order, and schedules the next frame. Describe how the run() method handles time-based operations like collision detection and throwable object creation. Explain the interaction between animation frames and update intervals, including how character movement and physics are updated on each render. Include performance considerations such as minimizing layout thrashing, efficient canvas operations, and garbage collection of unused objects. Provide examples showing how to debug loop timing and optimize for smooth gameplay. Address common loop issues like frame skipping, inconsistent update rates, and memory leaks from unbounded object arrays.

wiki_catalogs.name: World Class Reference
wiki_catalogs.prompt: Create comprehensive API documentation for the World class, which serves as the central game controller. Document all properties: character (Character instance), level (Level instance), canvas, ctx, keyboard, camera_x, throwableObjects (array), and lastThrow (timestamp). Explain the constructor's responsibilities: setting up rendering context, establishing world reference (window.world), and starting the game loop. Detail the draw() method's implementation using requestAnimationFrame for smooth rendering, including the coordinate translation for camera movement and the rendering sequence for background, UI, and game objects. Describe the run() method that drives game logic with setInterval calls. Document collision detection in checkCollisions() and projectile spawning in checkThrowableObject() with throwInterval() rate limiting. Explain the addToMap() and addObjectsToMap() methods that handle rendering with image flipping for left-facing entities. Provide examples of how the World coordinates all game systems and maintains object references. Include performance considerations for the multiple setInterval loops and potential optimization strategies.

wiki_catalogs.name: Input Handling
wiki_catalogs.prompt: Create comprehensive content for the keyboard input system that controls game interactions. Explain how the Keyboard class acts as a state container with boolean flags (LEFT, RIGHT, SPACE, UP, DOWN, ANY) representing key states. Document the event listeners in 1-game.js that update these flags on keydown and keyup events, including the special handling of the ANY flag that tracks whether any key is currently pressed. Describe how the World and Character classes poll this state rather than responding to events directly, enabling smooth continuous movement. Include code examples showing how arrow keys translate to character movement and spacebar triggers bottle throwing. Address common input issues such as key repeat limitations, simultaneous key presses, and cross-browser event differences. Provide solutions for improving input responsiveness and adding support for alternative input methods like touch or gamepad.

wiki_catalogs.name: Game Initialization Reference
wiki_catalogs.prompt: Create API documentation for the game initialization functions in 1-game.js. Document the init() function that sets up the game by creating a canvas element, retrieving its 2D context, instantiating the Keyboard class, and creating a new World instance. Explain the role of the body's onload event in triggering initialization. Detail the keyboard event listeners that update the Keyboard object's state properties on keydown and keyup events, including the special handling for preventing default browser scrolling with arrow keys and spacebar. Document the global variable declarations (world, keyboard, canvas, ctx) and their purpose in maintaining game state. Provide examples of how to modify initialization parameters or extend the setup process. Include troubleshooting guidance for common initialization issues like missing canvas elements or unresponsive controls. Explain the relationship between this initialization script and the World constructor's execution.

wiki_catalogs.name: Technical Reference
wiki_catalogs.prompt: Create API documentation for all public classes and methods in the el_polo_loco codebase. For each class (DrawableObject, MovableObjects, Character, Chicken, Endboss, Clouds, BackgroundObject, StatusBar, ThrowableObject, Keyboard, World, Level), document the constructor parameters, public properties, and methods with their signatures, parameters, return values, and purpose. Include detailed descriptions of key methods like Character.jump(), World.checkCollisions(), ThrowableObject.throw(), and DrawableObject.draw(). For functions like init() in 1-game.js, document invocation requirements and side effects. For level1.js, document the structure of the level configuration object including arrays for backgroundObjects, clouds, enemies, and statusBars. Specify data types, default values, and valid ranges where applicable. Include usage examples for each major API component. Address inheritance relationships and overridden methods. Provide guidance on extending existing classes and creating new game elements following the established patterns.

wiki_catalogs.name: Level Configuration Reference
wiki_catalogs.prompt: Create documentation for the level1.js configuration file that defines the first game level. Document the structure of the level1 object which instantiates the Level class with arrays of clouds, enemies, backgroundObjects, and statusBars. Detail each component array: backgroundObjects (repeating landscape images), clouds (parallax scrolling elements), enemies (Chicken instances at specific positions), and statusBars (StatusBar instances for health, bottles, coins, and endboss health). Explain the coordinate system and positioning logic used to place objects along the 2160px level. Provide examples of how to modify existing object positions or add new entities. Include guidance on creating additional level files following the same pattern. Document the relationship between this configuration and the Level class constructor parameters. Explain how the World object accesses this level data during game initialization and rendering.

wiki_overview.content:
<blog>
# Comprehensive Project Analysis: El-Polo-Loco Game

## 1. Project Introduction

El-Polo-Loco is a 2D side-scrolling browser-based game developed using vanilla JavaScript. The game features a character that navigates through a level, avoiding enemies and throwing projectiles to defeat them. The primary objective appears to be reaching the end of the level while maintaining health and defeating enemies, including a final boss.

Core goals of the project include:
- Creating an engaging 2D game experience using HTML5 Canvas
- Implementing game mechanics such as character movement, collision detection, and enemy AI
- Displaying game state through visual status bars

The target audience consists of casual gamers who enjoy simple, browser-based platform games with retro-style graphics and straightforward gameplay mechanics.

## 2. Technical Architecture

### Component Breakdown

The project follows an object-oriented architecture with a clear hierarchy of classes that extend base functionality. The game is built around a canvas-based rendering system with animation loops and event handling for user input.

```mermaid
classDiagram
    DrawableObject <|-- MovableObjects
    MovableObjects <|-- Character
    MovableObjects <|-- Chicken
    MovableObjects <|-- Endboss
    MovableObjects <|-- Cloud
    MovableObjects <|-- BackgroundObject
    DrawableObject <|-- StatusBar
    DrawableObject <|-- ThrowableObject
    MovableObjects <|-- ThrowableObject
    
    World --> Character
    World --> Level
    World --> Keyboard
    World --> ThrowableObject
    Level --> Enemy
    Level --> Cloud
    Level --> BackgroundObject
    Level --> StatusBar
```

### Design Patterns

The project implements several key design patterns:

1. **Inheritance Hierarchy**: A clear class inheritance structure where `DrawableObject` serves as the base class for all visual elements, and `MovableObjects` extends this with movement and physics capabilities.

2. **Game Loop Pattern**: The `World` class implements a continuous rendering loop using `requestAnimationFrame()` to create smooth animations.

3. **Observer Pattern**: Keyboard input is handled through event listeners that update a shared `Keyboard` state object, which is then polled by game objects.

4. **Composition**: The `World` class composes multiple game elements (character, enemies, status bars) rather than inheriting from them.

### System Relationships

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[HTML Page] --> B[JavaScript Game Logic]
    B --> C[World Controller]
    C --> D[Character]
    C --> E[Enemies]
    C --> F[Status Bars]
    C --> G[Background Elements]
    H[Keyboard Input] --> C
    C --> I[Canvas Rendering]
    I --> J[Browser Display]
```

### Data Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[User Input] --> B[Keyboard Event Listeners]
    B --> C[Keyboard State Object]
    C --> D[World Game Loop]
    D --> E[Character Movement]
    D --> F[Collision Detection]
    D --> G[Enemy AI]
    D --> H[Projectile Management]
    E --> I[Canvas Rendering]
    F --> I
    G --> I
    H --> I
    I --> J[Visual Output]
```

## 3. Key Implementation

### Main Entry Points
- `index.html`: The main HTML file that loads all JavaScript assets and initializes the game [index.html](c:\Users\DonSanchos\Documents\DEV-Local\Developer Akademie\Modul 12\el_polo_loco\index.html)
- `js/1-game.js`: Contains the initialization function and keyboard event handlers [1-game.js](c:\Users\DonSanchos\Documents\DEV-Local\Developer Akademie\Modul 12\el_polo_loco\js\1-game.js)

### Core Modules
- `models/2-world.class.js`: The central game controller that manages all game objects and the main game loop [2-world.class.js](c:\Users\DonSanchos\Documents\DEV-Local\Developer Akademie\Modul 12\el_polo_loco\models\2-world.class.js)
- `models/character.class.js`: The player character with movement, animation, and state management [character.class.js](c:\Users\DonSanchos\Documents\DEV-Local\Developer Akademie\Modul 12\el_polo_loco\models\character.class.js)
- `models/movable-objects.class.js`: Base class for all moving game objects with physics and collision detection [movable-objects.class.js](c:\Users\DonSanchos\Documents\DEV-Local\Developer Akademie\Modul 12\el_polo_loco\models\movable-objects.class.js)
- `models/drawable-object.class.js`: Base class for all visual elements with rendering capabilities [drawable-object.class.js](c:\Users\DonSanchos\Documents\DEV-Local\Developer Akademie\Modul 12\el_polo_loco\models\drawable-object.class.js)

### Configuration Approach
- `levels/level1.js`: Defines the game level with arrays of clouds, enemies, background objects, and status bars [level1.js](c:\Users\DonSanchos\Documents\DEV-Local\Developer Akademie\Modul 12\el_polo_loco\levels\level1.js)
- `models/keyboard.class.js`: Manages keyboard input state (though the file content wasn't provided) [keyboard.class.js](c:\Users\DonSanchos\Documents\DEV-Local\Developer Akademie\Modul 12\el_polo_loco\models\keyboard.class.js)

### External Dependencies
The project appears to be self-contained with no external package dependencies, relying solely on vanilla JavaScript and the browser's Canvas API.

### Integration Points
- HTML Canvas API for rendering graphics
- DOM events for keyboard input handling
- Browser animation frame API for smooth rendering

### Component Relationships

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[World] --> B[Character]
    A --> C[Level]
    A --> D[Keyboard]
    A --> E[ThrowableObjects]
    C --> F[Enemies]
    C --> G[Clouds]
    C --> H[BackgroundObjects]
    C --> I[StatusBars]
    B --> J[Animation States]
    K[Game Loop] --> A
    L[Keyboard Input] --> D
    D --> A
    A --> M[Canvas Rendering]
```

## 4. Key Features

### Functionality Overview

The game implements several core features:

1. **Character Movement**: The player character can move left and right, jump, and throw projectiles using arrow keys and spacebar.
2. **Physics System**: Gravity is applied to characters and objects, with proper ground collision detection.
3. **Animation System**: Different animation sequences for idle, walking, jumping, throwing, hurt, and death states.
4. **Collision Detection**: Rectangle-based collision detection between characters and enemies.
5. **Status Indicators**: Health, bottle, and coin bars displayed on screen.
6. **Parallax Scrolling**: Background elements move at different speeds to create depth.

### Implementation Highlights

**Game Initialization**: The game starts when the HTML body loads, calling the `init()` function which creates the World instance with the canvas and keyboard objects [1-game.js](c:\Users\DonSanchos\Documents\DEV-Local\Developer Akademie\Modul 12\el_polo_loco\js\1-game.js#L6-L11)

**Character Animation**: The Character class implements a sophisticated animation system that switches between different image sequences based on player state (idle, walking, jumping, etc.) and includes a "sleep" mode after periods of inactivity [character.class.js](c:\Users\DonSanchos\Documents\DEV-Local\Developer Akademie\Modul 12\el_polo_loco\models\character.class.js#L100-L150)

**Camera System**: The game implements a camera that follows the player character by translating the canvas context based on the character's x-position [2-world.class.js](c:\Users\DonSanchos\Documents\DEV-Local\Developer Akademie\Modul 12\el_polo_loco\models\2-world.class.js#L85-L87)

**Collision Detection**: The MovableObjects class implements pixel-accurate collision detection using offset rectangles to define hitboxes that are smaller than the visual representation [movable-objects.class.js](c:\Users\DonSanchos\Documents\DEV-Local\Developer Akademie\Modul 12\el_polo_loco\models\movable-objects.class.js#L30-L40)

**Status Bar System**: Status bars are dynamically updated based on character state, with the health bar width proportional to the character's energy level [status-bar.class.js](c:\Users\DonSanchos\Documents\DEV-Local\Developer Akademie\Modul 12\el_polo_loco\models\status-bar.class.js#L100-L110)

### Feature Architecture

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> GameInitialization
    GameInitialization --> GameLoop
    GameLoop --> PlayerInput
    PlayerInput --> CharacterMovement
    PlayerInput --> ProjectileThrow
    CharacterMovement --> CollisionDetection
    ProjectileThrow --> CollisionDetection
    CollisionDetection --> GameStateUpdate
    GameStateUpdate --> Rendering
    Rendering --> GameLoop
    CollisionDetection --> GameOver: Character energy = 0
    GameOver --> [*]
```
</blog>
